turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: We'll wait for the noise to subside, counsel. We'll wait for a moment or two, here. I think you may proceed whenever you are ready, now, Mr. Zell. was it not?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct. Mr. Wood was convicted in two counts and he received a $5,000.00 fine in each count, to run consecutively, and 12 months on probation. Therefore, he had a 24-month sentence and a $10,000.00 fine, payable again at the rate of $500.00 per month over a period of 20 months. Thereafter I appealed the case to the Georgia Court immediately when they were put on probation that they did not have the funds and the fines should be remitted or sus­ pended or some action taken, because there's going to be a problem in this case on this $20,000.00 in fines.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On your theory, then, the Court is power­ less to impose any punishments?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Oh no, the Court is certainly -- has

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that the fine was too high?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Correct. Well that, and commensurate with, first of all, the administration of justice -- what do you do with people who violate the law, and the judge, trial judge, makes a determination that these people can be released on the streets, in society, in public. They are not a danger, they shouldn't be incarcerated, they shouldn't be warehoused -- so to speak.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Zell, if you prevail here, what's going to happen: mandatory imprisonment in every case, of an indigent?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, I'd like to think, in our system of justice, that trial judges will not take that position. I'm sure some will. And I'd like to think that a trial judge--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm asking whether your argument might turn out to be counter-productive?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: It could, yes. I'm aware of -- some time sentences. I'm sure that's a possibility. stay out on bond pending this appeal, because they would have served their sentence if they had been incarcerated

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So they -- their sentences have been stayed?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And they have not been served?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What were the sentences? Or, how much jail time will they spend?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Mr. Tante received -- and Ms. Allen, received a 12-month sentence each. Under the computation, this is a high and aggravated misdemeanor; they would serve, they get four days --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I -- but the sentence was -- they were put on probation first, weren't they?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes. And they served almost three months of that sentence.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then --

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: They have nine left.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And was -- the probation was the same length as their sentences?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Nine months less good time, which is four days a month in this case; which would be 36 days less attitude and a different employer --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are these Petitioners here in forma pauperis ?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well doesn't that suggest some validity to Mr. Justice Blackmun's observation, that if the people are before the Court, but not for the first time or the second time, but for the fifth time, and the Court feels that, you know, we don't want to warehouse these people and lock them if they are indigent, and small -- or the small time fines haven't deterred them before, isn't there going to be a ten­ dency to at least send them to the -- some facility for confinement?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Okay, let me just relay a little chron­ ology. The second cases were imposed after this sentence. quit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well is that really.,a very commendable motive, that the employer pays their fine because they were convicted of doing something that was illegal under Georgia law?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well certainly you can't enforce any agreement between the employer and the employee, it would be against public policy, like enforcing a gambling contract; he promised to pay it, in some instances he did pay the fine. As soon as they left work, he didn't pay the fines.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is this a common provision in Georgia, between employees of so-called adult bookstores and their employers ?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes and no. It -- many times the employer has paid the fines for employees, many times -- many times he has not. I just filed a cert in this court last week involving a case called Simpson v. Georgia, in which he received a $20,000.00 fine in a different theater, different he was a short order cook. And the judge again revoked his worth?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the judge knew that he had enough money to pay that fine, didn't he?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well what happened --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Can I assume that?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: The judge knew -- well here's what happened, Your Honor -- being, as a practical matter, when they were convicted in the first instance, the judge, Judge nominal amounts of money as a clerk/projectionist. I'm sure the judge thought, it's rather obvious from the record, that he thought if he imposed this large fine their employer would pay it. I'm sure that was the thrust of why the fine was so heavy, was so large, your employer will pay it, based on maybe other -- other cases, or at prodding of the prosecutors, saying fine and dandy, don't worry, it will be taken care of.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the employer was just treating this as a cost of doing business, sort of a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- licensing fee?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: No. These people are the mere employees--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, the employees.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would happen if --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they're not working there anymore?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What would happen if the pre-sentence report shows that Mr. Jones has a friend by the name of Mr. Smith and Mr. Smith will give him a thousand dollars, and they fined him a thousand dollars, and they're not working together or anything, this is just a friend; would the Court be entitled

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On the fact that he used some bad infor- mation?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Certainly, he should, the Court -- again --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well when you get through, I want to know the difference between that case and this case.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well certainly he should -- the judge trial was

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You'd just let the judge go down that road?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct. What happened is, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now you want to complain about the judge?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, I mean that's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You're not complaining about yourself at all, are you?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, what happened is, the trial was over. The jury returned a verdict of guilty. At that point, the prosecutor -- and it's not in the record, but I can -- prodded the judge to impose a large fine. That normally is.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You still do?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Represent the third party?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: The defendants?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes. Oh, before this Court, yes, I do.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You were not a court-appointed counsel?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct, I was not.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he's here as an indigent, in forma

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: No. The defendants in this case, the way it came about, was, I received a call -- I- wrote the Court a letter, felt obligated to let the Court know that the defendants did not have the money after the appeal was ter­ minated, and they were no longer working for the owner of the theater, and I notified the Court that they did not have the funds and to please take some action, that they no longer worked in there and there was going to be some problem, they did not have the money and it would not be forthcoming. The Court chose not to take any action, this is in these three defendants and would I come down to court to represent them. I contacted the three defendants and I asked them, did they have money for counsel and did they want me to represent them, I would be glad to. And they said yes, we on probation their employment was terminated and therefore the ties were severed with the third party, or the employer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's clear, isn't it, Mr. Zell, that the monthly installment repayments were conditions of the probation?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was made clear that were these repay­ ments not made the probation would be revoked and the custod­ ial sentences would be imposed?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Okay. At that point, the fines were

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It was made clear that it was a condition of the probation?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Absolutely, yes. It was a condition of probation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, would it be -- and it's not con­ tended that the prison sentences imposed, which were probated stand on his head he would go to prison?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: I think that' s the reach in this case. It's just an unreasonable condition.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what, and let's say the judge knew at the time he imposed the probated sentence, that the defen­ dant, the convict didn't know how to stand on his head? If he could have imposed the prison sentence from the word go, couldn't he have imposed a -- an impossible condition?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: It becomes -- that's correct, it was an illusory sentence, gives them a.taste of liberty to- see what they could raise, and if you can't raise the money, in prison you go. It's really an illusory sentence. That's what I point out in my brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, from the point of view of the Defendants, isn't it better than a straight sentence from the beginning?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: No question about it. But what do you do when the judge makes a determination and assume he did, that these people should not be put in prison?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well but wasn't part of his determination scot-free?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it wasn't his decision, as indicated here, that they shouldn't be imprisoned if they paid $500.00 a month? HR. ZELL: That's correct, that's correct. Hoping that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Otherwise that they should be, that's the implicit condition.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: No question about it, that's correct. And all I'm saying before this Court is that when you have the situation, is that other alternatives should be made avail­ able to the Court, and for the Defendants, such as community service work, it would be much more constructive. How does it serve the administration of justice, the prison system; for example, in Georgia, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well we're not running the Georgia prison system. I mean, that's up to Georgia, to provide it, if it can -- if it falls within the United States Constitution.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes, but how does that help further the

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, I took the position in my brief, and I take it now, that in social and economic policies, it perhaps is not. But involving criminal justice, it should be. Certainly, for the purpose of having the money and the man go scot-free, such in this case, what if one of the proba­ tioners came up with the money? Mr. Wood, was able to raise the money, went back into business and that's open to him, perhaps, goes back in the adult book business, gets the money, and pays his fine; he goes scot-free, so to speak.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well you're suggesting that paying $5,000.00 is not somewhat painful.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well it's painful if you don't have it. I'm saying that if the person, they go and steal it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean it isn't painful if you have to go out and borrow it and put a mortgage on your house?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: It certainly is, if you have the financial think we have millions of people unemployed in this country, at the present time. I think it doesn't serve any purpose, any purpose, to lock up people who do hot have the money.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well if these people are in fact, indi­ gents as you suggest, putting them to work in community service instead of incarcerating them isn't going to enable them to support their families, is it?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, what you do Is, and this is just a suggestion, I mean not the only suggestion, is that if at nights -- there are many ways to serve the community, and much more productive for the community for the system, help the society in which they committed the wrong; it's cer­ tainly much more noble than to just lock somebody up behind bars for 7 or 8 months because they don't have the money. This case involves, and I'm sure the judge felt, and I conceded, that a third party would pay the money. And it has not been forthcoming. That's -- obviously the judge had that basis. This is similar in the Hunter v. Dean case, in and the question is, do you lock pepole up where they don't have the money? There should be other alternative means. This involves traffic cases, misdemeanors or felonies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't question, Mr. Zell, as I understand it, that the judge, so far as the Georgia at the outset -- prison sentences, without any probation whatsoever?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct. He made a determination they should be on probation at that point, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well if he made a determination they should be on probation if they paid $500.00 a month?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Oh no, no. Well, is this --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's a condition of the probation to be imposed?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes. They could go on probation, but they should pay $500.00 a month while they are on probation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well they will also go to jail if they don't live up to the other terms of their probation --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's right. QUESTION -- even if they pay the $500.00.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it isn't just a condition that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One of several conditions.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Right, that's -- which is in the appendix, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. you square this case with Tate case and the Williams case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yet, you concede that the judge could have sentenced them ab initio, to twelve months?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: In the very beginning.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct, that's correct. He felt that they were good subjects, fit subjects, to be on probation. That they should not be incarcerated -- he made that deter­ mination .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well but isn't that a typical determina­

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Generally speaking, Iwould say so, yes. The -- I might add that the American Bar Association of Standards relating to sentencing suggests -- I mean, the judge imposes a sentence, -- that he look at the profitability conditions and I'm also going to fine you $5,000, payable in installments and if you fail to pay the installments, you go to jail for ten days." That's a separate, I must have some way of -- something to take the place of the fine, but I'll just send you to jail for ten days if you don't pay the fine. But of course you are subject to going to jail for a year if you don't live up to the conditions of your probation. Would you be here then?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: I think so, yes. I don't think a man should serve one day in jail based on his wealth. I think that's -- should be a suspect category, if it isn't already under Griffin v. Illinois case. And I think that that should be a criteria. It serves no purpose to either raise revenue, punish the defendant -- because he sits in jail knowing that if he had the money, he'd be out, while other defendants and other clients I've had, have paid the fine and are not sitting in jail. I think it causes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying that dll criminal statutes that says 30 days or $100 are unconstitutional?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I don't know whether that's true or not; submission is that -- that $100 or one day is bad?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Only if the individual does not have the funds -- QUETION: All right, but that's bad to apply to any indigent?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Yes, or a person who has not squandered the money away, has not been negligent, willful; that seems to be the federal -- at least in the circuit, I'm from the Eiftn issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if he squandered the money, what difference does that make? no question about it; if he's incarcerated, and there's no would lie. If a man is put in jail for no reason, whether it be he has no funds or for whatever reason, the judge thought he ought to go to jail for no reason, he just didn't like the way -- his hair was too long -- I had a case like that recently, where the judge revoked a man's probation because his hair was too long. And I think I luckily reversed that in the state courts. But, a writ would lie -- you can't lock a man up for no reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what if a man were convicted of first degree murder and had no funds? get a life or death sentence. I’m only talking about where you' re placed .on . probation or given a some kind of a sus­ pended sentence and you use wealth, money, as the criteria to lock him up.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it isn't part of the trial judge's reasoning that you want to put as few people in jail as possible.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if he knew that you couldn't --

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Collect the fine?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- collect the fine or put him in jail, he -- he probably would sentence him to jail in the first place.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Well, I'm going to take it from a theor­ etical point of view, if I may. I'd like to think that trial judges will make a determination -- and I think they are duty- bound to do this -- whether a man should go to jail or not, we saying the government doesn't have the money to run itself? Is this a revenue-producing measure? Or what are we furthering by saying you pay this money, it's important in the system of criminal justice. It isn't important; it's very unimportant to collect money. And the judges, trial And to use money as a criteria to put a man in jail among murderers perhaps -- and he sits in jail and he says golly, my friend paid the fine and I can't. And he goes free because he had money. I think this is a discredit to our system of justice and it should not be allowed, and I think you should follow the Tate and Williams case, .this Court should, and did not permit this.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what it comes down to is to find a punishment that is available for imposition on him and I suppose some people, due to circumstances, are free from any punishment except going to jail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would you object if the Court had a hearing to find out if this man could pay $10.00 a week?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Not at all. In fact, --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I thought so, but you weren't saying it right then.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: In fact I asked the judge, I said look judge, reduce the fine, let's make it commensurate. This is a -- failure to pay as directed, $500 a month. There's nothing with the judge saying, well, I misread this, I thought you could pay $500 -- I'll reduce it to $10.00 a month, which may be as much to this individual defendant as $1,000 a week would be to some other defendants who are perhaps involved in a Waco conviction -- organized crime : conviction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your time is up. But just one question, at least from me. Pursuing Justice Blackmun's question, could the judge under Georgia law have said, I will consider proba­ tion if you will post a bond to guarantee the payment of your armed robbery, came before the judge for sentencing. Under your going-around-in-circles-argument, would it be a consti­ tutional defense for him to say I just didn't have any money, robbery?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: I'm. .sure many defendants have said that to the trial judge; I certainly wouldn't advise this Court that I was represented to say that. All we're saying is that after the judge makes a determination that he could be on the street, that he could be on probation, and he has a suspended sentence; don't lock him up later for not having wealth, not having material goods.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Zell, I don't want to repeat myself, but the determination that the sentencing judge made was that he would continue him on probation so long as he paid $500.00 per month.

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It wasn't an unconditional determination. money, that's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Among other conditions?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: That's correct. It was conditioned on his wealth, on money, and he could not -- he reached the other other conditions of probation, there were about ten of them in the record. But the condition he couldn't reach was the wealth, the money condition, and that shouldn't be a criteria or reason to lock a man up.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And other people who could satisfy that stayed out of jail?

turns.name: GLENN ZELL
turns.text: Who could pay the money, could stay out of jail. And if the Court determines that -- that this man paid the money and stayed out -- then that would be a reverse discrimination, then the Courts say well maybe they could do soipe community service work, put in a lot of hours, some of your time is worth the minimum wage, $3.00 an hour, and work on weekends or at nights, working this fine off in the community. tion probation on the payment of a fine, revoke that period of probation for non-payment of the fine, particularly when the individual has represented to the Court that they had the funds or the means by which to pay the fine. Underlying that consideration is whether the sen­ assisting the individual in the payment of the fine. Now this case does not involve, as some cases pre­ viously to the Court have provided, extending the period of confinement greater than allowed by statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Attorney General, suppose a man has got the money and he is sentenced to pay $5,000 a month, which is no problem to him. But he starts serving the sentence at the end of the year 1929 and he is caught along with everybody else. In Georgia, what happens? is there any relief for him?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes, he can always go through it in Georgia, at any time, the Courts -- we pointed that out in the footnotes, the Court always has the inherent power to Now, I think it's important in this case that the under the misapprehension, and I'll correct that, on page 9 did not, as Mr. Zell said, initially. I think the important factor is that these individuals are out on bond, on an appeal bond, that bond was posted on February 13, 1979, which was the date when the judge said that after the hearing in January, of '79, if you don't pay the arrears, I'm going to give you two weeks to pay the arrearage for approximately $1500, then I'm going to revoke probation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the amount of that bond?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Now the bond for Mr. Wood is $20,000, and under the Georgia statutory scheme, the maximum amount -- or, the amount that would have to be paid for that bond, according to my figures, would be $1,025. The statute is Georgia Code 27-501. That sets out the fact that you can't charge any more than 10 percent of the first five hundred indicate where this indigent got the money to pay that?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: No, and I think that gets into --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well if it's not in the record, I don't waht you to pursue it. I just wanted to know if it was in the record

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: I think the interesting part of the A, which is the revocation proceeding in January of .1979, Mr. Zell told the sentencing judge, the trial judge, Judge Alexande that these individuals' previous fines in connection with other cases, had been paid and that he had paid them. Now, -- I think —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You said previous fines?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Perhaps I misundertsood Mr. Zell, but I thought that this was their first conviction.

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: All right. If I can, the scenario is, this is their first conviction. This is what happened: each one of them was arrested and they pursued their .consti­ tutional right to submit the matter before a jury; a jury in Now, after that time, they appealed to the Court of Appeals, then to the Supreme Court of Georgia, then a writ of certiorari. In the interim, between the time of the October 23, 1978 order when the judge says all right, I'm going to -- the terms and conditions of probation start on this date. They entered pleas of guilty to subsequent offenses. It was those subsequent offenses which are referred to in our Appendix A on the brief in opposition to writ of certiorari, where Mr. Zell tells the judge yes, I paid the fine, or the employers gave me the money to pay these fines --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the could the judge have revoked the then-pending probation because of that conviction of a subsequent crime?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Yes, he could have, I believe -- well, I believe he could have. Well, I think the important part is, of the case, is that he had -- and what we're talking about here is sentencing discretion of a trial judge. What may he consider? Can he consider other things? Now, in the United States v. Grayson, this Court said when he said you know, I find your defense to be highly incredible and you know, basically, I think you're putting the sham on the Court. By analogy, cannot the trial judge con­ sider representations to him by third parties that we will pay the fines? Now, what does this do in the mind of the trial judge? He says yes, we're going to put you, I'm going to give you 12 months, and a $5,000 fine. Or the 12 months and $5,000 authorized by law.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What does the record show, if anything, about who paid the bond or the fee of $1,000, or more?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The bond for the -- continued liberty, while this case is --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Well I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't there something in --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice Burger, it was not until the 27th of October that I found out that these indi­ viduals had a bond posted. Now perhaps I was negligent in Now in the appendix, there is indication that the King Bonding arrested -- and, I would assume, the continuing appeal bonds until October 23rd, 1978.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Attorney General, if that's true, why wasn't the owner arrested?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Justice Marshall, I have no idea, and I cannot speak for the solicitor of Fulton County.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I understand.

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Now these individuals also indicated -- and I think the representation is, too, properly to the Court, that here, the employer has -- the employer even told one of them, I believe it was Mr. Tante, there was an indi­ cation in the probation revocation hearing, well how did you know to get Mr. Zell? I believe there is testimony in there that he had represented them previously. And I believe there's also testimony in that revocation hearing that the -- it had been suggested by the employer to contact Mr. Zell. And there's order of probation, "It is further ordered that the Court, and the Defendant is hereby advised that the Court may at any time revoke or modify any conditions of this probation or change the period of probation, and may discharge the Defendant from probation." Was any application made in this case to discharge the Defendant from probation?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: None on the record, now. And I say that with -- well for the qualified remark, that on page 71, of the probation revocation hearing, there is an indication from Mr. Zell that I wrote the court. But I think as we pointed out in our brief in this case, at the time the sen­ tences were imposed, the Defendants -- Petitioners in this case -- sat back in silence. They never went in to the proba­ tion officers at that time and said, you know, we can't pay. There's an indication that the probation officer said we'll accept partial payments, and they never went in and told the -- indicated to their probation supervisors that you know, we their employer, and say, are you going to pay the fine, the record is silent, they did nothing. They never, at any time, went and tried to pay the fine. There's no showing that they went out to see if they could borrow any money, to my understanding. All of them were earning some sort of money and and the word indigent, making $525 a month, another one $400-$500 a month, and the one lady was on unemployment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did they ever ask the Court to reduce the amount of the installments and put them over a longer period of time?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: No, there was no effort made at any time to reduce the installments or to seek anything else. As a matter of fact, on page 69 of the Appendix -- Appendix A, which is not the brown appendix, but the one appended to our didn't ask the question because I thought I could enforce the agreement. Right there, there's evidence right there that the judge was under the impression that the third parties would pay this fine. Now why should the judge be able to look to third parties? In the probation context, just as if we're putting the individual out on parole, we're trying to find an alternative to confinement. Now an alternative to confinement has the purpose of rehabilitation. We want to deminimize the amount of super­ look to a citizen, an employer or somebody in the community, sentencing judge. Now, Mr. Zell and Petitioners say well gee, that was an illegal agreement. That is, illegal to the extent that you pre-suppose that the individual was hired with the purpose of violating the law. But I think the judge rationally could look to the employers because if they put a stake up in the employment. Now one of the terms of the probation was that they continue on employment. If that individual is working he can contribute to his family. He doesn't become a burden to society. The collection of fine in this case is not like Tate v. Short, where they said it's a revenue measure. The judge in this case is looking to third parties because they are going to help in the rehabilitative aspect of that individual. And also --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't the judge a part of the whole thing?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: A party?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How can a judge take the word of a man who is the criminal? we're reading too much into it, because that assumes that the solicitor knew that the employer was going to pay the fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought you said that that was stated in open court.

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: I don't believe that the solicitor stated it in open court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't say the solicitor, I said some­ body did.

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Right, and that was the Petitioners--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the solicitor --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: -- in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- was there, wasn't he?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So he heard it, didn't he? Justice Marshall and the Court, is that when an employer in this situation says that he will vouch for this individual -- particularly rehabilitative efforts -- is he not telling the Court that he is going to see to it that his conduct conforms to the law?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would it be the same as a dote?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Would it be the same as --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That the King tendered a dote says to the Court that I'll pay the fine for these guys?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would that be all right?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: I think you could say, because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Would that be all right?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Isn't that an indication that he's got a stake in this individual and that he's going to conform his conduct to the law?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you think the Court would go along with that?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Well I don't know, but I think it's that if somebody's going to pay some money out for them, are Appeals just said --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what difference does it make about

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Right. And --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that your position?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: That's correct. And one of the increasing the sentence at all. The sentence was --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: --you know, 12 months, $5000 fine. He could, and as Mr. Justice Rehnquist said and you said earlier, he could have slap, right off the board, said, you know, in you go and $5,000 fine.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what if the sentencing judge had known at the time he imposed the -- as a condition of probation -- that the convicted defendants repay $500 a month, what if he'd known at that very moment that there was no possibility at all that the defendants could repay?

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Well that question is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that just the equivalent --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: -- a little more, that's a little

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Well I agree, I think it makes it to the judge and I don't think, on the record here, there's anything to impune lack of judicial integrity on the part of the sentencing judge. I don't think we can go that far, to say that. And then we're hypothesizing and going to all sorts of realms that are not in the confines of this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Attorney General Dunsmore, I got the impression from your adversary that the problem presented by this case is somewhat unique to Georgia. Do you have that same impression? And if so, --

turns.name: JOHN W. DUNSMORE, JR.
turns.text: Well I'm not too sure of -- when he said unique to Georgia -- I take it, in terms of the fact that judges don't place individuals on, let's say, spend five nights in the county jail or work for a health facility. I don't think that's unique, because I have -- and this is outside the couldn't tell you that. But I don't think that it -- you know, that it's unique, because I do know from some limited experience outside, that you know, other alternatives, depend­ ing on who the judge is. But what we're getting back to, or what I believe we need to get back to is:, the sentencing dis­ cretion of the judge and the fact that other alternatives are available or he could consider other alternatives, is it unconstitutional to consider financial resources, and is it unconstitutional to enforce the terms of the sentence which is prescribed by law? And that's exactly what we're faced with, and we say it's not. And we say, particularly, that it's not, because there is some element of deception in this case. It was, Mr. Justice Marshall said, you know, leading the judge down the path. I, as attorney, and even as a citizen, would find that somewhat disturbing and irreprehensible that somebody sits down and misinterprets or misleads the Court, particularly in the context that we're brought up -- particularly the aura of the Court -- I think most criminal defendants and most individuals, when they are faced in a court situation, tend to be honest. Now, and I'm not supposing this case that the defendants were, you know, were less than honest, but they didn't need to speak up and say the third party is paying the I said that after 19 months, particularly on October 23, 1978, or even during that period of time, why didn't they go to their attorney and say look, there's just no way we can pay it. Why wait three months until the solic­ itor goes in and says I want an order to revoke your probation? Where is the good faith effort on their part? And I think North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 79-6027 Raymond Wood, et al. v. Georgia and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court.
